## [03:00 - 06:01]

So for all those who don't know, first of all, you have a pixel. Every single ad platform has a pixel that is simply put accumulating data that's sent back to it. The simple way to articulate a pixel is that it's used for reporting to be able to successfully demonstrate when somebody off of the platform that you used to send the traffic somewhere else got a result for you by doing so. So if I'm using Facebook and Instagram or TikTok or Google AdWords or LinkedIn or whatever ad channel, when I send that person off of the platform somewhere else, this is just simply a way for other websites to communicate back to the ad channel that a result happened off of the ad channel. Simple enough, right? And that is definitely a function of the pixel. But what's more powerful to understand about the pixel is that the data that comes back to it heavily influences and dictates who your ads are probable to target next. It works in waves, what we call prediction batches. So these prediction batches, you can also look at like buckets, okay? And when you have a bucket that's full of good quality people and then you got some buckets later on that are full of not so good people, let's number these buckets one, two and three. And let's say bucket one is some qualified people. Let's say bucket number two is some unqualified people. And then let's say over here that bucket number three is a mix of both. And let's use the example that each one of these buckets has a total of 100,000 people in it. Now, when you are targeting anybody, whether it's lookalike audiences, interest stacks, broad targeting, customer lists, warm audiences, you do not reach everybody within that given audience. You reach the people that are the most probable to convert. So every pixel has something referred to as a standard event. And this is what you select when you optimize a campaign. Some examples of standard events. If you run a call funnel, you might use something like schedule or complete registration. Some of you are using lead. Shouldn't be doing that, but we'll talk about that shortly. You have lead, which is great for opt-in funnels, very surface level actions. You have purchase, which is great. You can also send back the amount that somebody's spending when they purchase something to further help optimize campaigns. I'll talk about some conspiracies associated with that here shortly. Now, there's plenty of other standard events that exist, some of which you'll never use and that you'll never optimize for. But to give you a comprehensive example, there are standard events like search, donate, app install, view content, submit application, schedule, complete registration, purchase, add to cart, initiate checkout, and several others. Some of which are specific to ad channels and some of which are used universally by all ad channels. You also have outside of standard events, what's known as a custom conversion. Custom conversions are business defined events. They can be linked to a standard event, but in some instances you can signal a custom conversion occurred without linking it to a standard event.
